front: Opera: verismo opera by Ruggero Leoncavallo about a commedia dell'arte troupe, almost always paired with Cavalleria rusticana
Opera: ends with Canio stabbing Nedda and her lover Silvio, followed by the declaration 'La commedia è finita!'
Opera: opens with a prologue in which Tonio, dressed as Taddeo, addresses the audience with 'Si puo?' and reminds them the performers are 'men... like you'
Opera: its Intermezzo echoes the baritone solo that opened it
Opera: an Act I Bell Chorus has villagers singing 'don, din, don' to imitate vesper bells, preceding Nedda's ballatella
Opera: Nellie Melba performed as Nedda in the 1893 Covent Garden premiere
Opera: its tenor aria's 1902 recording by Enrico Caruso was the first record in history to sell over a million copies
work: "Vesti la giubba" – Canio's Act I Aria

front: Opera: Canio sings 'Vesti la giubba' at the end of Act I as he puts on his clown costume, after discovering his wife's infidelity with Silvio
work: "Vesti la giubba" – Canio's Act I Aria

front: Opera: 'Vesti la giubba' commands its singer to 'turn your distress and tears into jest, because people have paid money and want to be amused'
work: "Vesti la giubba" – Canio's Act I Aria

front: Opera: Nedda sings the ballatella 'Stridono lassù' about birds in flight; she imitates birdsong and afterwards fends off Tonio's advances with a bullwhip

Opera: the closing line 'La commedia è finita!' was delivered by Victor Maurel (who also sang the prologue) in the original 1892 production; in Zeffirelli's 1982 film, by Juan Pons rather than Placido Domingo
Opera: after 'La commedia è finita!', the score calls for three strokes on a tam-tam (marked 'T.T.')
